event: Early Archaic (pre-ceramic) Amerindian peoples settle Martinique, among the first human inhabitants of the island.
Arawak-speaking Saladoid peoples migrate from South America and establish ceramic-making agricultural villages on the island.
Saladoid Arawak communities flourish, cultivating cassava and producing distinctive painted pottery.
Kalinago (Island Carib) peoples become dominant, displacing or absorbing earlier Arawak populations.
Christopher Columbus reportedly sights Martinique during his second voyage, though he does not land.
Columbus lands at Martinique on 15 June during his fourth voyage, encountering the Kalinago inhabitants.
Pierre Belain d'Esnambuc establishes the first permanent French settlement at Saint-Pierre, claiming the island for France.
King Louis XIII authorizes the use of enslaved Africans in the French Caribbean colonies.
French colonists wage war against and largely exterminate or expel the remaining Kalinago population.
Martinique comes under the control of the French West India Company, intensifying sugar plantation development.
Martinique becomes a direct possession of the French Crown after the dissolution of the West India Company.
France's Code Noir is promulgated, regulating slavery across the French colonies including Martinique.
British forces capture Martinique during the Seven Years' War.
The Treaty of Paris returns Martinique to France in exchange for territorial concessions elsewhere.
Britain occupies Martinique, and the island's planters resist the abolition of slavery decreed by revolutionary France.
The Treaty of Amiens restores Martinique to France, where slavery had never been abolished on the island.
Britain again seizes Martinique during the Napoleonic Wars.
Martinique is definitively returned to France under the Treaty of Paris.
Slavery is abolished in Martinique, freeing tens of thousands of enslaved people following Victor Schoelcher's campaign.
Indentured laborers from India begin arriving to work the sugar plantations after emancipation.
Citizens of Martinique gain the right to elect a deputy to the French National Assembly under the Third Republic.
The eruption of Mount Pelee on 8 May destroys Saint-Pierre, killing some 30,000 people in one of the deadliest volcanic disasters of the century.
Aime Cesaire, poet and founding father of the Negritude movement, is born in Basse-Pointe.
Martinique falls under Vichy French control after the fall of France, leading to economic hardship and blockade.
Martinique rallies to the Free French forces, ending Vichy administration on the island.
Martinique becomes an overseas department (departement d'outre-mer) of France, with Aime Cesaire among the architects of departmentalization.
Frantz Fanon, the influential anti-colonial philosopher born in Fort-de-France, begins his career; he becomes a major intellectual figure of the era.
Riots erupt in Fort-de-France amid social and racial tensions, leaving several dead.
Martinique becomes a French administrative region in addition to its departmental status.
Martinique benefits from decentralization reforms granting greater regional self-governance within France.
Patrick Chamoiseau, a leading voice of the Creolite literary movement, wins France's Prix Goncourt for the novel Texaco.
Martinique marks the 150th anniversary of the abolition of slavery amid renewed debate over colonial memory.
The euro replaces the French franc as Martinique's currency.
Aime Cesaire dies in Fort-de-France and receives a state funeral as a national figure.
A general strike over the high cost of living paralyzes Martinique for weeks, echoing wider unrest in the French Caribbean.
Voters reject greater autonomy in a referendum but approve merging the department and region into a single territorial collectivity.
The new single Territorial Collectivity of Martinique replaces the separate departmental and regional councils.
France acknowledges responsibility for the chlordecone pesticide contamination that poisoned Martinique's soil and population.
Statues of Empress Josephine and other colonial figures are toppled by activists protesting slavery's legacy.
Protests against COVID-19 health measures and a mandatory vaccine policy spark unrest across the island.
Violent protests over the high cost of living grip Martinique, prompting a curfew and negotiations with French authorities.
